title: Japanese stab binding, asanoha
subtitle: The hemp-leaf stab-stitch pattern
excerpt: Asanoha toji, the hemp-leaf stitch, is the second-most common Japanese stab-binding pattern. It uses the same four base holes as the yotsume four-hole stitch but adds diagonal wraps between them that form a six-pointed star at each hole cluster. The result is a denser, more decorative thread pattern on the spine.
sourceNotes: Asanoha toji documented in late-Meiji Japanese papercraft and bookbinding manuals (Internet Archive, ja.archive.org, Meiji-era craft collection). The hemp-leaf pattern is one of three canonical stab-stitch decorative patterns in the tradition alongside kikko (tortoiseshell) and yotsume (four-hole). Cross-referenced with Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), appendix on Eastern bindings.

glossaryTerms.definition: The hemp-leaf Japanese stab-binding pattern: asanoha (hemp leaf) + toji (binding). Named for the six-pointed star that the diagonal thread wraps form at each hole cluster.
A binding in which holes are pierced through the entire text block side-on, and thread is sewn through them at the spine margin. Pages open to roughly 90°.

content.text: by adding a second thread pass that laces diagonals between the base holes, forming a six-pointed star at each hole cluster. The base structure uses the same four holes as yotsume; the hemp-leaf pattern uses six holes per cluster: two at each base position plus additional positions between them. For a readable A5
, use two clusters (head and tail), with the diagonal wraps linking them.

attrs.title: Practice the four-hole stitch first
attrs.body: The asanoha pattern is significantly clearer to execute once the four-hole yotsume is familiar. Sew at least one four-hole booklet before attempting the hemp-leaf variation.

items.name: Pre-assembled text block, A5 or smaller, bulldog-clipped at the foredge
Waxed linen bookbinding thread, 4-cord, contrasting colour optional
items.qty: Approximately 5× the height of the book

items.name: Metal ruler and pencil for marking hole positions

content.text: For an A5 text block (210 mm tall), mark six holes from the head: holes A (5 mm), B (18 mm), C (35 mm) from the head; and holes D (35 mm), E (18 mm), F (5 mm) from the tail. Holes C and D are the inner limits of the two clusters; B and E are the middle hole of each cluster; A and F are the outermost. Pierce all six holes through the entire block from front to back.

Begin at hole A from the back. Enter front to back at A, leaving a 10 cm tail at the front. Loop around the head edge and back through A (anchor wrap). Run along the front face to hole B. Enter B back to front.

Loop around the spine edge at B and back through B. Run along the front face to hole C. Enter C back to front. Loop around the spine and back through C. Run to hole D; enter D back to front. Continue to E and F in the same pattern, looping at each.
At hole F, loop the tail edge as with the head at A. Now work the return path: back through F, along the spine to E, through E, along to D, through D, along to C. At C begin the diagonal wraps.

From C, angle the thread diagonally along the spine to hole B, passing over the C-to-D thread. Enter B from the back. Angle diagonally from B to hole A; enter A from the back. Loop around the head edge again and back through A. Angle from A diagonally back to B; enter B from the front. Angle from B diagonally to C; enter C from the front. The six-pointed star around the head cluster is now formed.
Repeat the diagonal sequence at the tail cluster (holes D, E, F). Tie off at A with two half-hitches around the initial tail. Trim both tails to 5 mm.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems that arise in asanoha toji.

items.symptom: The diagonal wraps cross each other in the wrong order and the star pattern looks tangled
items.cause: The diagonal wraps must always pass over the main spine thread, not under it, to form the star correctly.
items.fix: Draw a diagram of the six holes before sewing and mark the over-under crossing at each diagonal junction. In practice: when bringing the thread diagonally back across the main spine thread, keep the diagonal on the outside (front face) of the book.
